title: Control, authority and trust
summary: 9609 A Level — authority, control systems, trust culture, and balancing control with empowerment.
learningObjectives: Define authority and types of control.
Explain role of trust in organisations.
Analyse trade-off between control and initiative.

simpleExplanation.title: Rules vs empowerment
simpleExplanation.summary: Authority is legitimate power to decide; controls ensure objectives are met. Trust speeds decisions and boosts morale — but too little control risks fraud and quality failure.
steps: Authority = right to give orders and expect compliance.
Financial budgets and quality standards are key controls.
Trust improves morale, speed, and cooperation.
Excessive control stifles initiative and innovation.

flashcards.back: Legitimate organisational power attached to a role.
Lower monitoring cost, faster decisions, better teamwork.
Clear targets + autonomy within limits + audit.
Harder direct supervision — output-based controls needed.

sections.content: **Control, authority and trust** (7.1.4) — evaluate **case culture**: start-up may need **more control** as it scales; mature firm may need **more trust** to innovate.

sections.question: Retail bank introduces strict call monitoring and script compliance after mis-selling scandal. Sales fall but complaints drop. Evaluate the control–trust balance.
sections.solution:
**Need for control:** **Mis-selling** harmed customers and **regulator fines** — tight scripts **reduce legal risk**.

**Cost:** **Scripts kill upselling** and **employee autonomy** — sales fall; **Herzberg motivators** lost (2.2.3).

**Trust path:** **Training on ethics**, **commission reform**, **sample audits** instead of **100% scripting**.

**Verdict:** **Necessary tightening** short term; long term rebuild **trust-based culture** with **smarter controls** not only surveillance.

sections.label: Mark a control and trust question
